full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Under normal circumstances, I would have had my annual Maundy Thursday pedicure by now, and I would be delivering this homily surrounded by basins of water because we would be in church together, practicing the very thing Maundy Thursday is named for.<|18.50|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus' mandatum novum, or new commandment,<|5.00|> <|7.44|> that he demonstrates not just in word, but in deed.<|11.18|> <|12.22|> He disarms his closest followers,<|14.66|> <|14.66|> not through a persuasive discourse,<|17.40|> <|17.40|> but through wrapping a towel around his waist,<|21.04|> <|21.04|> placing their feet in basins of water,<|23.52|> <|23.52|> and washing their feet.<|25.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This is God's prerogative in Christ,<|4.40|> <|4.40|> to take on flesh and to serve God's creation from a place of overflowing humility.<|12.56|> <|12.56|> But how is this humility, this service,<|16.40|> <|16.40|> this act of self-donation to take place amidst a global pandemic<|22.16|> <|22.16|> that is only stemmed through abstention from physical touch and proximity?<|28.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In personal planning and in team meetings<|4.18|> <|4.18|> for our Holy Week liturgies,<|6.50|> <|6.50|> I have mulled over this question with no answer in sight.<|10.50|> <|11.62|> How are we to wash one another's feet virtually?<|16.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This question follows the existential question so many of us were faced with just over a month ago.<|8.24|> <|9.24|> How are we to receive Jesus' body and blood in Holy Eucharist if social distancing, not social proximity,<|18.28|> <|19.28|> is the sensible, hygienic, and wise path forward?<|24.68|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> it.<|1.22|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I give you a new commandment, that you love one another.<|5.10|> <|6.08|> Just as I have loved you, you also should love one another.<|10.42|> <|11.58|> By this, everyone will know that you are my disciples if you have love for one another.<|17.24|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> These are Jesus's words to his disciples and by extension us.<|5.16|> <|5.90|> And what is the loving act in this season of global pandemic?<|11.46|> <|12.64|> It is remaining home, if at all possible, and mitigating the spread of coronavirus.<|20.32|> <|21.56|> It is not only keeping our health and well-being in mind, but the well-being and health of those we love in mind.<|28.86|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is doing everything in our power to love others as Jesus loves us.<|6.42|> <|7.38|> It is in our context, not washing the feet of other people.<|13.62|> <|14.84|> On this Maundy Thursday, we refrain from the physical contact and proximity<|20.32|> <|20.32|> that will put ourselves and others at risk for infection.<|24.86|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> If, as one scholar has said, justice is what love looks like in public, then this historic<|10.06|> <|10.06|> moment brings home that, quote, individual behavior is important for collective safety,<|19.40|> <|19.40|> end quote, as said by Dr. Valerie Beemo.<|24.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Through social distancing, we are taking seriously our love for anyone at risk of contracting this virus.<|11.06|> <|12.36|> On this Maundy Thursday, Jesus' Mandatum Novum, or new commandment, is to stay at home and pray for those we love but will not see for a season.<|25.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is to be kind to those delivering food to us in isolation or those still stocking shelves in our grocery stores and markets.<|9.48|> <|11.10|> The commandment we call new is not so new, is it?<|16.38|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is quite ancient insofar as it finds its origins in God's love for all creation down to the plants that sprout forth in Genesis.<|11.74|> <|12.34|> The people God so faithfully guided through the wilderness and exodus and the nation formed through traumas of exile and return all throughout the Hebrew Bible.<|23.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> While the commandment may be in fact old, as we try it on year after year, it becomes new to us.<|9.68|> <|10.52|> Because love is not a stale, stagnant thing.<|15.12|> <|16.02|> Instead, love is not a stale, stagnant thing.<|17.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The love of which Jesus speaks and the love he demonstrates tonight is a love that adapts and remains flexible,<|8.74|> <|9.34|> even as our needs and desires evolve over the course of our lives.<|15.38|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Yes.<|1.02|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Some time ago, a friend sent me Dan Albergati's poem, "Things to do in the belly of a well,"<|9.60|> <|9.60|> a fitting piece for these times when we must love in ways we never thought imaginable before.<|16.56|> <|16.56|> He begins by saying this, "Measure the walls."<|23.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Count the ribs.<|2.64|> <|2.64|> Notch the long days.<|5.08|> <|5.08|> Look up for the blue sky through the spout.<|8.20|> <|8.20|> Call old friends and listen for echoes of distant voices.<|13.10|> <|13.10|> Dream of the beach.<|15.32|> <|15.32|> Review each of your life's 10 million choices.<|19.50|> <|19.50|> Try to be very quiet and listen for the sound of gears<|24.26|> <|24.26|> and moving water.<|25.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Listen for the sound of your heart, end quote.<|4.84|> <|6.62|> Listen for the sound of your heart, even in the belly of the well,<|12.54|> <|13.06|> even amidst the precautions of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.<|18.34|> <|19.62|> Listen for the sound of your heart, and you might hear love.<|25.78|> <|27.06|> Amen.<|27.44|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|1.20|><|endoftext|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> These are Jesus's words to his disciples and by extension us.<|5.16|>
<|5.90|> And what is the loving act in this season of global pandemic?<|11.46|>
<|12.64|> It is remaining home, if at all possible, and mitigating the spread of coronavirus.<|20.32|>
<|21.56|> It is not only keeping our health and well-being in mind, but the well-being and health of those we love in mind.<|28.86|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is doing everything in our power to love others as Jesus loves us.<|6.42|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> If, as one scholar has said, justice is what love looks like in public, then this historic<|10.06|>

<|19.40|> end quote, as said by Dr. Valerie Beemo.<|24.10|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Through social distancing, we are taking seriously our love for anyone at risk of contracting this virus.<|11.06|>
<|12.36|> On this Maundy Thursday, Jesus' Mandatum Novum, or new commandment, is to stay at home and pray for those we love but will not see for a season.<|25.60|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> While the commandment may be in fact old, as we try it on year after year, it becomes new to us.<|9.68|>
<|10.52|> Because love is not a stale, stagnant thing.<|15.12|>
<|16.02|> Instead, love is not a stale, stagnant thing.<|17.20|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The love of which Jesus speaks and the love he demonstrates tonight is a love that adapts and remains flexible,<|8.74|>
<|9.34|> even as our needs and desires evolve over the course of our lives.<|15.38|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Yes.<|1.02|><|endoftext|>
